Someone stole some money from your coworker's purse when she left it on the desk in her office. She tells you about this. You can't believe it. You say: I can't believe someone would do such a thing. When someone who you like and respect does something bad that surprises you, you use this phrase. For example, if your husband yells and says something mean to your sister, you would tell him: I can't believe you would say that to her! In that situation, you can also say: I can't believe you said that! But there is a small difference between the two. "I can't believe you would ___" sounds more surprised and hurt. You can also use "I can't believe you (did something)" for a surprising positive action. For example, if your wife bought you a really amazing and expensive birthday gift, you could say: I can't believe you got me a motorcycle! If you said "I can't believe you would get me a motorcycle", it would definitely sound like you were angry at her. "Such a thing" means "a thing like that". It's mostly used in two totally different situations. One is in sentences like the one at top. Another example: How could you do such a thing? This is a sentence that you say when someone does something horrible to you. The other situation where "such a thing" is used is in the expression "There is such a thing as ___": Is there such a thing as love at first sight? This means "Does love at first sight exist?" or "Is it real?" The negative version of this is "there's no such thing". Notice that it doesn't include "a" before "thing": I remember being really disappointed when I found out there was no such thing as Santa Claus. Some musicians and composers have a form of synesthesia that allows them to "see" music as colors or shapes. This is called chromethesia. Mozart is said to have had this form of synesthesia. He said that the key of D major had a warm "orangey" sound to it, while B-flat minor was blackish. A major was a rainbow of colors to him. This may explain why he wrote some of his music using different colors for different music notes, and why much of his music is in major keys. [1w On seeing a box of matches] Stepping Stones Reading Words As of yet, in our Stepping Stones reading class, we have covered the letter sounds/recognition for: m, a, s, d, t, i, n, p, g, o, c, k, u and b. We are moving at a fast pace, so please practice all sounds and recognition of these letters. Overall, the children are doing very well! We have even already begun to Stretch and Read simple two and three-letter words! Feel free to also add any other 2 or 3-letter word combinations which the children can sound out. We call words that we can sound out "GREEN" words. Please practice! We have also begun to learn sight words. These are words the students cannot sound out and have to learn by memory/sight! We also call them "Popcorn" or RED words. These are the following words we have learned so far: I, me, look, a, my. Please practice at home also! Thank You! Help a Child Choose a Book |Grades||K - 6| |Tip Topic||Tips for Reading To or With Kids See all tips in this series Why Use This Tip Children become good readers one book at a time. But how do you help a child choose the right books? You don't have to be an expert in reading levels to guide a child to books that entertain, enlighten, and challenge (without overwhelming). You may be accustomed to choosing books for the children in your life. But did you know that selecting a book is a useful skill that a child can and should learn? Choosing a book independently teaches a child that we seek books for different reasons. With some simple strategies, you can help a child to be a savvy book selector. You also can help him or her choose books that are neither too easy nor too hard. Much like Goldilocks found when she crashed at the three bears' house, the books a child picks can be just right! - As early as you can, introduce the idea that we read for a purpose, even if that purpose is pure enjoyment. Ask the child: What type of book are you looking for today-and why? With older children you can ask: Are you looking for fiction (made up) or nonfiction (factual)? Ask them to explain their choice. - Encourage a child to spend time browsing a selection of books at a library or bookstore. If this is overwhelming try organizing the books you already have at home and letting your child browse through them. You can separate them by fiction (made-up stories) and nonfiction (factual). Or you can put them in different bins by ability level or type (picture books, chapter books, and so on). Talk about the type of book in each container. - Give the child authority over choosing books to read. Say "yes" as often as you can. A book that the child wants to read is the one you want to take home. Don't worry if a book seems short, too easy, or has pictures. Graphic novels (stories told in comic book frames) are a great way to hook a reluctant reader. And looking at pictures is a perfectly acceptable way to read a book. - Let the child know it's OK if he or she doesn't like a particular book. Use a not-so-great selection as an opportunity to understand more about reading skills and preferences. Could it be the book was just a little too difficult for the child to tackle alone? Here's a simple rule of thumb for younger readers: Let them keep track of how many words they didn't know. If they count five or more on a page, the book was probably too challenging for an independent read. Use the Was This the Right Book for Me? worksheet to encourage a more detailed thumbs up or thumbs down. - If the child really wants to read something you know is beyond his or her ability, solve it by reading it aloud together. You can take turns reading and define unfamiliar words as you go. That way the child will avoid the frustration and enjoy the added bonus of your company! - If you'd like to know more about what's recommended for a child's age and grade, visit your local library or bookstore. Browse the sections, which usually group books based on the reader's skill level. Ask questions there, too. And look at a book's back cover for info about the age the publisher had in mind. A child's teacher or school librarian can be a great source, too. Not only do these folks know what's age-appropriate, they usually know the hot books among kids. If they can't keep it on the library shelves, chances are your child will like it! - If the child has really enjoyed a book, look for other books by the same author. - Additional online resources for books include: I think it's easier if you put one leg in each pajama pant instead of both in the same one. Show me you have to show mom. Look here's some more shakes [toy rattle]. [6 sec D snaps picture at 757; S squeals at 8 - reaction to flash] What else are they having for dinner? Hey d' you think that baby wants a drink? Handling a mean mom can be a challenging task, but with the right approach, it is possible to improve your relationship with her. Here's a step-by-step guide to help you deal with a mean mom: **Step 1: Understand Her Perspective** The first step in handling a mean mom is to try and understand where she's coming from. It's important to recognize that your mom might be going through something stressful or difficult, which could be causing her to act out. By putting yourself in her shoes, you'll be better equipped to respond calmly and empathetically. Key Tip: Avoid taking things personally. Remember that your mom's behavior may have nothing to do with you and everything to do with her own struggles. **Step 2: Communicate Openly** Communication is crucial when dealing with a mean mom. Express your feelings honestly, but tactfully. Use "I" statements instead of "you" statements to avoid sounding accusatory. For example, say "I feel hurt when you speak to me harshly," rather than "You always hurt my feelings." Guideline: Choose an appropriate time and place for this conversation. Make sure both you and your mom are calm and receptive to what the other person has to say. **Step 3: Set Boundaries** Setting boundaries is essential when dealing with a mean mom. Let her know what behaviors are unacceptable to you and stick to those limits. This will help create a healthier dynamic between you two. Key Tip: Be firm yet respectful when setting these boundaries. Explain why certain behaviors bother you and propose alternative ways of interacting. **Step 4: Seek Professional Help** If your relationship with your mom continues to deteriorate despite your efforts, consider seeking professional help. A therapist or counselor can provide guidance and support as you navigate this complex relationship. Guideline: Look for a mental health professional who specializes in family dynamics or maternal relationships. They'll have the expertise needed to assist you effectively. **Step 5: Practice Self-Care** Handling a mean mom can take a toll on your emotional wellbeing. That's why it's vital to prioritize self-care. Engage in activities that bring you joy and relaxation, such as exercise, meditation, or hobbies. Surround yourself with supportive friends and family members too. Key Tip: Don't isolate yourself. While it's important to spend time alone, make sure you also maintain connections with others who uplift and encourage you. **Step 6: Stay Patient** Improving a strained mother-child relationship takes time. Stay patient and persistent, even if progress seems slow. Keep communicating openly, setting healthy boundaries, and practicing self-care. Over time, these strategies should lead to positive changes. Guideline: Celebrate small victories along the way. Every instance of improved communication or reduced conflict marks significant progress towards healing your relationship. Remember, handling a mean mom requires understanding, patience, and resilience. But by following these steps, you're one step closer to fostering a healthier, more loving connection with her. An oboe is a woodwind instrument with a double reed. It looks very similar to the clarinet, and may be confused with it. While the clarinet's shape remains cylindrical, the oboe's body is conical. The sounds produced by clarinets and oboes are very different. An oboe's sound is produced by blowing air through the double reed at the upper end of the instrument which forces the two reeds to vibrate together which produces the sound. The oboe has four parts: the bell, lower joint, upper joint, and the reed. A person that plays the oboe is called an oboist. A typical orchestra may have two oboes but sometimes three. Sometimes there is also a cor anglais which sounds a fifth lower than the oboe. Very occasionally there is also a bass oboe, which sounds an octave below the oboe. Gustav Holst used one in his Suite "The Planets". Once upon a time, there was a little girl named Lily. She loved to play with her toys and go outside to play. Title: How to Choose a Wiki Package Introduction: A wiki is a website that allows users to add, modify, or delete its content via a web browser. It is often used as a knowledge sharing platform within organizations or by communities interested in collaborating online. With many wiki packages available today, choosing one can be overwhelming. This tutorial will guide you through selecting a suitable wiki package based on your needs. Step 1: Identify Your Needs Determine what you want from your wiki. Is it for internal company use? Or do you plan to open it up to external contributors? What kind of features are essential - version control, user permissions, file uploads, etc.? Knowing these requirements will help narrow down your options. Key Tip: Make sure to consider scalability if you anticipate significant growth in usage or data volume over time. Step 2: Research Available Options There are numerous free and paid wiki solutions. Some popular ones include MediaWiki (used by Wikipedia), DokuWiki, Tiki Wiki, Confluence (by Atlassian) and Twiki. Each has unique strengths and weaknesses, so research thoroughly before deciding. Guideline: Look at factors such as ease of installation, system compatibility, customization capabilities, community support, security features, and pricing models when comparing different packages. Step 3: Evaluate Ease of Installation & Setup Consider whether you have the technical skills to install and configure the software yourself or if you'll need professional services. If you lack the necessary expertise, opt for a solution with straightforward setup procedures or comprehensive documentation. Key Tip: Many providers offer cloud-based versions of their products which can eliminate the hassle of manual installation. Step 4: Check System Compatibility Ensure the chosen wiki runs smoothly on your existing hardware and software environment. Most modern wikis require PHP and MySQL, but specific versions might differ between applications. Step 5: Assess Customizability Check if the interface can be tailored to match your brand identity. Also, look into whether there's an extension or plugin system that lets you enhance functionality without modifying core code. Step 6: Review Community Support & Documentation Strong community backing usually means quicker problem resolution and more active development cycles. Good documentation also makes learning curves less steep. Both aspects contribute significantly to overall user experience. Step 7: Analyze Security Features Security should be paramount. Investigate how user authentication works, whether encryption is supported, and if regular updates are provided to patch vulnerabilities. Step 8: Understand Pricing Models Free doesn't always equate to better value. Consider total cost ownership including any potential hidden charges like premium feature fees or maintenance costs. For commercial options, weigh up the price against included benefits and ROI prospects. Step 9: Test Drive Demos / Trials Where possible, test out demos or take advantage of trial periods. Hands-on experience provides valuable insights unattainable through mere reading. Step 10: Seek Feedback From Users Reach out to current or past users to gather feedback about their experiences. Their testimonials could reveal pros and cons overlooked during initial evaluations. Conclusion: Selecting the right wiki package involves careful consideration of various factors ranging from functionalities needed to budget constraints. By following this detailed tutorial, you're now equipped to make informed decisions ensuring optimal results. And here's a little girl see? "Le Dernier jour d'un condamn " ("The Last Day of a Condemned Man") had a big influence on later writers such as Albert Camus, Charles Dickens, and Fyodor Dostoevsky. "Claude Gueux" appeared in 1834. It is a documentary short story about a real-life murderer who had been executed in France. Hugo himself considered it to be a precursor to his great work on social injustice, "Les Mis rables". One of the effects of the novel was to make the inhabitants of Paris restore the neglected Cathedral of Notre Dame, which was attracting thousands of tourists who had read the popular novel. Wet weather was the worst; the cold, damp, clammy wet, that wrapped him up like a moist great-coat-the only kind of great-coat Toby owned, or could have added to his comfort by dispensing with. Wet days, when the rain came slowly, thickly, obstinately down; when the street's throat, like his own, was choked with mist; when smoking umbrellas passed and re-passed, spinning round and round like so many teetotums, as they knocked against each other on the crowded footway, throwing off a little whirlpool of uncomfortable sprinklings; when gutters brawled and waterspouts were full and noisy; when the wet from the projecting stones and ledges of the church fell drip, drip, drip, on Toby, making the wisp of straw on which he stood mere mud in no time; those were the days that tried him. Then, indeed, you might see Toby looking anxiously out from his shelter in an angle of the church wall-such a meagre shelter that in summer time it never cast a shadow thicker than a good-sized walking stick upon the sunny pavement-with a disconsolate and lengthened face. [sounds like sound made by moving lips with side of finger] Title: Understanding Divisibility with the Help of Powers of Four Have you ever heard your teacher talking about divisibility rules in math class? It's a fun and easy way to figure out whether a number can be divided evenly by another number like 2, 3, 5, or 9. In this article, we will explore a cool property of powers of four and learn why none of them are divisible by three. You don't need to know advanced concepts like linear algebra or calculus; just basic arithmetic skills will do! Imagine having marshmallows representing different numbers. Let's say our marshmallow friend "four" wants to join its friends in a row to show off its powers ($4^n$). But wait! There's a rule - no odd numbers of marshmallows are allowed because we're working with powers of four today! So, let's see what happens when we start lining up those fluffy pals: * One marshmallow: $4^1 = 4$, looks good! * Two marshmallows: $4^2 = 16$, still fine! * Three marshmallows: $4^3 = 64$, hmm... notice something strange happening already? The sum of digits is now divisible by three! This breaks our rule since the power of four cannot have an odd number of marshmallows. Let's investigate further using modulo arithmetic - think of it as taking turns counting on your fingers starting with zero after each count. We say two numbers belong to the same remainder class if their difference gives a multiple of the base number. For example, in the world of threes (base 3), both 4 and 1 share the same remainder class because $4 - 1 = 3$, which is indeed a multiple of three. Check this out: * Modulo 3: $4 = 1$; so, $4^n = 1^n$ due to our special friendship between fours and ones in the land of threes. No matter how many times you multiply one together, it remains one! Great job sticking around through these marshmallow tales! Now you understand that even though it may seem tempting to include an odd bunch of marshmallows while building towers of fours, nature has other plans. Each time you add more layers, you end up getting a digit sum that eventually becomes divisible by three, making it impossible for any power of four to be an actual multiple of three. [Nina becomes interested in playing with the village again.] Title: How to Care for American Shorthair Cats American Shorthairs are medium-large cats known for their short coat, muscular build, and friendly personalities. They have a lifespan of around 15 years with proper care. Here's an extensive guide on providing optimal care for your American Shorthair cat. **Step 1: Provide Proper Nutrition** American Shorthairs need balanced nutrition to maintain good health. Feed them high-quality commercial cat food or consult with a vet about home-prepared diets. A diet rich in proteins (meat), vitamins, minerals, and low carbohydrates is ideal. Avoid people food as some can be harmful to cats. *Key Tip*: Always provide fresh water daily. **Step 2: Regular Vet Visits** Regular veterinary checkups help detect any potential issues early. Kittens should visit the vet every three weeks until they're four months old, then annually after that. Adult cats over seven years old may require biannual visits due to age-related risks. *Guideline*: Spaying/neutering should be done between four to six months of age unless breeding purposes exist. **Step 3: Grooming** Despite being short-haired, these cats shed seasonally. Weekly brushing reduces hairballs and distributes natural oils throughout the fur. Pay attention to behind the ears, under the legs, and tail areas where matting often occurs. *Key Tip*: Start grooming from kittenhood so they get used to it. **Step 4: Dental Hygiene** Dental disease affects many cats. Brush teeth weekly using feline toothpaste and finger brush or pet toothbrush. Offer dental treats and toys designed to reduce plaque buildup. *Guideline*: Yearly professional dental cleanings might be necessary depending on oral condition. **Step 5: Exercise & Playtime** These active felines benefit from playtime which stimulates mental and physical activity. Use interactive toys, lasers, balls, etc., ensuring safety. Create vertical spaces like shelves or trees for climbing. *Key Tip*: Schedule regular exercise time to bond with your cat. **Step 6: Maintain Litter Box Hygiene** Clean the litter box regularly to prevent bacterial growth and odor problems. Most cats prefer uncovered boxes filled with clumping clay litter. Location matters; place it in a quiet but accessible spot. *Guideline*: One box per cat plus one extra is recommended. **Step 7: Socialization** Socializing your American Shorthair from young age makes them friendlier. Gradually introduce new experiences, people, animals, and environments. Never force interactions if your cat seems uncomfortable. *Key Tip*: Positive reinforcement training methods work best. **Step 8: Environmental Enrichment** Providing enriching surroundings prevents boredom and stress. Add scratching posts, window seats, hiding places, and puzzle feeders. Rotate toys to keep things interesting. *Guideline*: Make changes based on your cat's behavior - if they seem anxious or lethargic, reassess their environment. By following these steps, you will ensure your American Shorthair lives a happy, healthy life while strengthening the human-animal bond. Remember, individual needs vary among cats, so always observe and respond accordingly. [Yiddish 'what are you doing with your feet', 1 sec] [Chi notices that there are more of the silver men in the plastic tray.] Discharge Instructions: Taking Your Pulse Taking your pulse is a way to measure your heart rate. When you take your pulse, you are feeling the force of blood as it's pumped from your heart into other parts of your body. You may need to take your pulse regularly. Or you may just need to take it when you exercise or when you feel something is wrong. 1. Find Your Pulse With your first 2 fingers (index and middle fingers), press lightly on the inside of your wrist, just below the base of the thumb. You should not be pressing on a bone. The beats you feel are your pulse. If you can't find your pulse, try moving your fingers slightly to a new spot. 2. Take Your Pulse Count the beats you feel in your wrist as you watch the second hand on a clock. You may be told to count the beats for 6 seconds and then multiply that number by 10. Or you may be told to count for a full minute. The number you get is your pulse measurement. It is measured in beats per minute (bpm). A normal pulse is between 60 and 100 beats per minute. The beats should be regular (evenly spaced). 3. Write Down the Results Write down your pulse each time you take it. You may be asked to bring the results with you each time you visit the doctor. A dozen of eggs a dozen of eggs a dozen of eggs. Something untrue is false. A half truth is something true mixed with something false, or something partly true with key information omitted. Get some tape and some floor space to play this fun game with your child. Use some masking tape and make a big tic-tac-toe board on the floor or rug. Then find 3 toys of the same thing (three cars, blocks, teddy bears) and 3 socks of the same color. Then let your toddler place a toy or sock in each square. First see if she can place an object in each square. Then after she is done with all of them. Go over what's in each square. If your child happened to put three of the same items in a row, celebrate and tell her what a great job she did!. Then have her take them all off and let you put them down. You can also repeat with taking turns, almost like a real game. - masking tape - Have fun and giggle each time your child gets three in a row! To drive safely, you must be able to see everything within the danger zones around your bus. You can see everything if your mirrors are adjusted correctly. Working with the station monitor, use the diagram on the next page to place 16 traffic cones around the bus. These traffic cones will outline the danger zones. The diagram indicates how far each cone should be from the bus. When placing the cones, measure from the side of the tire to the center of the cone. Once you have placed the cones, sit in the driver s seat of the bus. If your open door blocks your vision, close the door. Have the station monitor adjust your mirrors so that you can see all the cones, either with the mirrors or with the naked eye. Students will identify what sound is and make musical instruments out of 8 glass bottles 1. Take a soda straw and pinch one end flat. 2. Cut off the corners to form two little reeds. The fact that it's cuttable means you can make it into an oboe. 3. Hold the reeds in your mouth without squeezing them. 4. Now blow hard. 5. Carefully cut small holes along the length of the straw about one inch or so 6. Blow in the straw while you cover and uncover the holes to play simple The two reeds opening and closing at high speed first allow air to flow into the straw and then stop the flow. The vibrating air is what produces the sound. l. Take 8 glass bottles and fill each bottle with a little more water than the 2. Take a spoon and tap each bottle to hear a different sound. When you tap each bottle, it makes the bottle vibrate. The pitch of the note depends on the amount of water in the bottle. The pitch of note is lower with l. Take a shoebox and cut a hole in the top. 2. Place several large rubber bands of various widths around the box to make a The plucking of the rubber bands causes them to vibrate producing various sounds. The thicker the band, the lower the note. The thinner the band, the higher the note. 80% accuracy is successful. Herbert, Don, Mr. Wizard's Supermarket Science, Random House, NY You want to put the bug on your finger? This section looks at some skills you will need as you start to learn about algebra. It starts with some work on codes, then moves on to work with letters and formulae. Remember that the outer ring contains the uncoded letters and the inner ring contains the coded letters. If we see an expression which contains these letters, we need to replace the letters by their corresponding numbers to find the value of the expression. Look at the examples below: (a) Find the value of 6 + b:| Replacing b by 7 gives 6 + 7. The answer is 13. (b) Find the value of 2a + b:| Remember that 2a means 2 a. Replacing a by 4 and b by 7 gives 2 4 + 7. The answer is 15. (c) Find the value of ab:| Remember that ab means a b. Replacing a by 4 and b by 7 gives 4 7. The answer is 28. (d) Find the value of a(b - c):| You need to know that a(b - c) means a (b - c). Replacing a by 4, b by 7, and c by 3 gives 4 (7 - 3). The answer is 16. Calculate the value of each expression below and then to see whether you are correct. In this question, a = 2, b = 5 and c = 1. (a) What is the value of b + 5? (b) What is the value of 3a - c? (c) What is the value of ab? (d) What is the value of b(a + c)? (a) Simplify 2x + 5x. 2x is the same as x + x. 5x is the same as x + x + x + x + x. Therefore, 2x + 5x must be the same as (x + x) + (x + x + x + x + x), which is 7x. (b) Simplify 9y - 5y. We have 9 lots of y and we are taking away 5 lots of y. This will leave 4 lots of y, so 9y - 5y = 4y. (c) Simplify 3a + 8b + 5a - 2b. We can only collect together the terms which have the same letter (the like terms). We have 3a and 5a which add together to make 8a. We also have 8b and we are subtracting 2b, which will give 6b. The 8a and 6b are added together to give 8a + 6b. (a) Simplify 3p + 5q. We can not combine these terms because they contain different letters. Therefore, 3p + 5q is already simplified. Simplify each expression below, and then click to see whether you are correct. (a) Simplify 2x + 4x. (b) Simplify 5p + 7q - 3p + 2q. (c) Simplify y + 8y - 5y. (d) Simplify 3t + 4s. |We want to work out formulae for the area and perimeter of the rectangle.| To find the area, we multiply the length (x) by the width (y).| This is x y which is the same as xy The formula is Area = xy To find the perimeter, we add up the lengths.| This is x + y + x + y which is the same as 2x + 2y The formula is Perimeter = 2x + 2y Um did you see any XXX or did you notice anything different from last time? You were going to try out for it then you weren't sure you were? Title: How to Perform a Classic Coin and Pen Trick Difficulty: Easy to Moderate **Things You'll Need:** 1. A regular coin (preferably a quarter or similar sized coin) 2. A pen with a clickable top (a ballpoint pen works best) 3. Sleight of hand skills (developed through practice) Step 1: Familiarize Yourself With the Props Before attempting this trick, spend some time getting comfortable handling both the coin and the pen. Practice flipping the coin between your fingers and thumb, as well as spinning the pen around using different grips. This will help you become more adept at manipulating these objects and make the overall trick easier to perform. Key Tip: The smoother your movements are, the less likely audiences are to suspect any deception. So take the time to hone your fine motor skills. Step 2: Master the Grip Hold the pen vertically between your thumb and index finger, with the clip facing downwards. Position the coin so that it rests flat on top of your middle fingertip. Make sure that about half of the coin is hidden behind your ring finger, which should be slightly curled inward. Your pinky finger should also be tucked under the coin for added support. Guideline: Maintain a firm but relaxed grip throughout the entire trick. If your hold is too tight, it may look suspicious; if it's too loose, you risk dropping the coin or pen accidentally. Step 3: Conceal the Coin Transfer In one swift motion, push the concealed portion of the coin against the backside of the pen while simultaneously clicking the pen's top button. As you do this, quickly turn your hand over so that the pen is now pointing upwards and the coin has been transferred onto the other side of the pen. All of these actions should appear seamless and fluid. Key Tip: Timing is crucial here. Practicing the transfer slowly before speeding up can ensure you get the hang of it without revealing the secret move. Step 4: Show Both Sides After successfully transferring the coin, show your audience both sides of the pen - first the end where the clip is attached, then the opposite end. Remember to maintain a natural and casual demeanor during this process. Act as though nothing unusual is happening, even though you just performed a magic trick! Step 5: Reverse the Process Now it's time to bring the coin back to its original position. To accomplish this, reverse the steps taken in Step 3. Start by holding the pen upright again, hiding the coin partially behind your ring finger. Then, slide the coin off the pen and onto your middle fingertip while clicking the pen's top button once more. Once completed, display the coin in its starting spot to conclude the trick. Troubleshooting Tips: - If the coin falls off during the transfer, try applying a bit more pressure when pushing it against the pen. - If the pen doesn't click loudly enough to mask the sound of the coin moving, consider practicing with a louder pen or finding ways to distract your audience momentarily. - Always remember to keep your hands steady and movements smooth to avoid raising suspicion. By following these steps and practicing regularly, you'll soon master the classic coin and pen trick. Happy performing! Title: How to Change the Text Tone on Your iPhone Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through changing the text tone on your iPhone. This feature allows you to customize the sound that plays when you receive a new message or notification. By following these steps, you can make this process easy and enjoyable while adding a personal touch to your device. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Make sure your iPhone is updated to the latest iOS version before making any changes. * Familiarize yourself with the layout of your iPhone's settings menu to ensure a smooth experience. * Remember that any changes made during this tutorial may be reversed by returning to the same menus and selecting different options. Step 1: Accessing Settings Navigate to your home screen by pressing the round button at the bottom center of your phone (also known as the 'Home' button). Once there, locate and tap on the grey gear icon labeled 'Settings'. Explanation: The first step requires entering the main system preferences area where all the settings are stored. Here, you can modify various aspects of your iPhone according to your needs and requirements. Step 2: Select Sounds & Haptics In the Settings app, scroll down until you find an option called 'Sounds & Haptics', then select it. It should be located between 'Accessibility' and 'Privacy.' Explanation: You need to access the specific section dedicated to managing sounds and vibrations produced by your iPhone upon certain events such as receiving messages or emails, placing calls, locking screens, etc. Step 3: Choosing Text Tone After tapping 'Sounds & Haptics,' look for the category named 'Text Tone.' Underneath it, you'll see several pre-installed alert sounds like 'Chime,' 'Bubble,' 'Glass,' etc., along with their corresponding icons. Explanation: Each type of event has its own set of available alerts; here we are focusing only on adjusting the text tone. These preset tones vary in pitch, rhythm, and melody so choose one that resonates with you. Step 4: Previewing Sound Options Tap on any of the listed audio files under 'Text Tone' to hear what they sound like. After playing a tone, if you decide it isn't suitable, simply tap another one to listen to it instead. Explanation: Before finalizing your selection, previewing the sounds gives you an idea about which tone suits best based on your preference. There's no limit to how many times you can switch between them until you settle on one. Step 5: Saving Changes Once satisfied with your chosen text tone, press 'Back' at the top left corner of your screen twice - once to exit from 'Text Tone' submenu and again to return to the main 'Sounds & Haptics' page. All modifications made within this session get automatically saved without requiring explicit confirmation. Explanation: Confirming your choice involves navigating back out of the nested menus structure to save the newly selected text tone as default. Now, whenever someone sends you a message, your iPhone will play the customized tone rather than the standard one. Conclusion: Congratulations! You have successfully changed the text tone on your iPhone! Feel free to explore other settings categories and learn more ways to personalize your device. Always remember, every small change contributes to enhancing user experience and creating a unique identity for your smartphone. I should be in I want to stay. The earliest written evidence of chess is found in three romances (epic stories) written in Sassanid Persia around 600. The game was known as "chatrang" or "shatranj". When Persia was taken over by Muslims (633-644) the game was spread to all parts of the Muslim world. Muslim traders carried the game to Russia and to Western Europe. By the year 1000 it had spread all over Europe. In the 13th century a Spanish manuscript called "Libro de los Juegos" describes the games of "shatranj" (chess), backgammon, and dice. Title: How to Become a Legal Adult in the United States Introduction: In the United States, turning 18 marks the transition from being a minor to becoming a legal adult. This milestone comes with new rights and responsibilities that can significantly impact your life. Here's a comprehensive guide on what you need to do to become a legal adult in the US. Step 1: Understand What It Means to Be a Legal Adult Explanation: At 18, individuals gain many new rights such as voting, entering into contracts, getting married without parental consent, joining the military, and more. However, this also means they are now responsible for their own actions and decisions. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Familiarize yourself with both the privileges and obligations associated with adulthood. - Consider seeking advice from trusted adults or legal professionals about these changes. Step 2: Register to Vote Explanation: One of the most important responsibilities of an adult is participating in democratic processes. You can register to vote once you turn 18; however, deadlines vary by state so make sure to check yours well before elections. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Visit usa.gov/register-to-vote to find out how to register in your state. - Remember that some states require proof of identity when registering. Step 3: Apply for a Government-Issued ID Explanation: A government-issued photo ID like a driver's license or non-driver identification card verifies your age, identity, and residence. These are often necessary for tasks like opening bank accounts, applying for jobs, or boarding airplanes. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Check your local DMV website for application requirements and fees. - If you already have a driver's permit, update it to a full license after gaining enough driving experience. Step 4: Get Medical Insurance and Start Regular Checkups Explanation: As a legal adult, healthcare becomes primarily your responsibility. Make sure you have health insurance coverage either through your employer, parents (until age 26), Medicaid, Medicare, or private insurers. Regular medical check-ups help maintain good health and detect potential issues early. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Explore options under the Affordable Care Act if uninsured. - Schedule regular appointments with primary care physicians and dentists. Step 5: Manage Finances Independently Explanation: Being financially independent involves understanding credit, managing debt, filing taxes, budgeting, and saving for retirement. Opening a checking account, creating a budget plan, and setting financial goals are excellent starting points. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Learn basic personal finance skills online or at community centers. - Avoid unnecessary debts and spend wisely. Step 6: Know Your Rights and Obligations Under the Law Explanation: Understanding laws related to employment, housing, consumer protection, privacy, etc., will protect you from exploitation and ensure ethical behavior. Also, learn about criminal law since ignorance isn't accepted as an excuse. Key Tips & Guidelines: - Read up on federal and state laws relevant to your situation. - Consult with attorneys or legal aid services if unsure about any aspect of the law. Conclusion: Becoming a legal adult signifies greater freedom but equally larger responsibilities. By following these steps meticulously, you can smoothly navigate this significant phase of life. Title: How to Draw Cartoons in Adobe Animate (Formerly Flash) Introduction: In this extensive guide, you will learn how to create your own cartoon characters from scratch using Adobe Animate, formerly known as Flash. This powerful software allows artists, designers, and animators to bring their ideas to life through vector-based animation. By following these steps meticulously, you'll be able to craft unique and engaging cartoon creations that can be used in various multimedia projects such as web design, video games, or animated films. Step 1: Setting up Your Workspace * Launch Adobe Animate and create a new document by clicking File > New. Choose "Scene" under Document Type, set the width and height according to your preference, and select RGB Color at 72 pixels/inch resolution. Click OK. * Familiarize yourself with the interface. The Tools panel is located on the left side containing essential tools like Brush, Pencil, Rectangle, Oval, Text, etc. On the right side, there are Properties, Layers, and Timeline panels where you control properties, manage layers, and animate objects respectively. * Customize your workspace if necessary. Go to Window >Workspace >Essentials to ensure all basic windows are visible. You may also choose other predefined workspaces based on your needs. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Organizing your workspace effectively increases productivity and reduces clutter. * Use custom keyboard shortcuts for quick access to frequently used tools. Step 2: Sketching Basic Shapes * Begin by sketching rough shapes using the Brush tool (B), Pencil tool (P), or any shape tool (Rectangle Tool (R), Oval Tool (O)) in a new layer named "Sketch". Lock this layer before proceeding further so you don't accidentally modify it while working on details. * Experiment with different colors for easier visualization during the drawing process. To change color, simply pick another hue from the Fill color box at the bottom of the Tools panel. * Keep refining until you have a clear outline of your character. Don't worry about perfection; focus more on proportions and overall structure. Key Tips & Guidelines: * Practice makes perfect! Regular practice improves speed and accuracy when creating complex forms. * Break down complicated structures into simpler components for easy manipulation. Greek interest in bringing back the Olympic Games began with the Greek War of Independence from the Ottoman Empire in 1821. It was first proposed by poet and newspaper editor Panagiotis Soutsos in his poem "Dialogue of the Dead", published in 1833. Evangelis Zappas, a wealthy Greek-Romanian philanthropist, first wrote to King Otto of Greece, in 1856, offering to fund a permanent revival of the Olympic Games. Zappas sponsored the first Olympic Games in 1859, which was held in Athens. Athletes from Greece and the Ottoman Empire participated. Zappas funded the restoration of the ancient Panathenaic stadium so that it could host all future Olympic Games. Once upon a time, there was an old squash in the garden. On Friday, he ate through five oranges, but he was still hungry. Charging a Ring Doorbell Follow these steps to charge your Ring doorbell: 1. Check the battery level: Before charging your Ring doorbell, you should check its current battery level. This will help you determine if it's necessary to charge it immediately or if you can wait until the battery is almost depleted. To do this, open the Ring app on your smartphone and select your doorbell from the list of devices. The battery level will be displayed as a percentage. 2. Gather necessary materials: Once you have determined that your Ring doorbell needs charging, gather the following materials: - A USB cable (specifically the one provided with your Ring doorbell) - A power source (such as a laptop, wall outlet, or portable charger) Key tip: Make sure to only use the USB cable provided by Ring, as using an unauthorized third-party cable may damage the device. 3. Remove the security screws: Turn off your Ring doorbell by pressing and holding the button on the back of the device for 20 seconds. Then, locate the two hidden security screws at the bottom of the faceplate. Use the appropriate screwdriver (either a Phillips head or a flathead, depending on the model) to remove them. 4. Detach the faceplate: Carefully lift the faceplate away from the device once the security screws have been removed. Be gentle during this process so as not to damage any components. 5. Locate the battery compartment: On the inside of the Ring doorbell, there should be a small battery compartment near the top of the device. It might be covered by a removable rubber tab. If present, gently pull the tab away to reveal the charging port. 6. Connect the USB cable: Insert one end of the USB cable into the charging port located on the Ring doorbell. Plug the other end into a power source such as a computer, wall outlet, or portable charger. The LED light on the front of the Ring doorbell should turn on when connected properly. 7. Monitor charging progress: Keep an eye on the Ring app to monitor the battery's charging status. You can also view the battery icon directly on the screen of the Ring doorbell itself. When fully charged, the battery indicator will show 100%. Depending on the model, charging could take between four to six hours. 8. Disconnect the USB cable: After the Ring doorbell has finished charging, safely disconnect the USB cable from both ends. Reattach the rubber tab over the charging port if applicable. 9. Attach the faceplate: Place the faceplate back onto the Ring doorbell, aligning the connectors correctly. Firmly press down on all sides to secure it in place. 10. Secure the faceplate: Using the same screwdriver used earlier, replace the security screws on the bottom edge of the faceplate. Tighten them firmly but avoid excessive force to prevent damaging the screw holes or the device. Congratulations! Your Ring doorbell is now successfully charged and ready for use again. Regularly checking and maintaining the battery life will ensure optimal performance of your device. Title: How to Have Fun When No One is At Home Introduction: Staying home alone can be an opportunity for relaxation, self-care, or indulging in hobbies that you may not have time for when others are around. This guide will provide you with various activities and steps to help make your solo time enjoyable, productive, and safe. Step 1: Create a Safe Environment Before engaging in any fun activities, ensure that your home environment is secure. Here's what you should do: * Lock all doors and windows. * Make sure emergency exits are accessible. * Familiarize yourself with important phone numbers (emergency services, neighbors, family members). * Keep your cellphone charged. * Set up timers for lights if staying indoors after dark. * If possible, let someone know about your plans and estimated return time. Key Tip: Your safety should always come first! Step 2: Plan Ahead Decide beforehand what kind of activity suits your mood. Would you like some quiet time reading? Or perhaps physical exercise through dancing or yoga? Planning ahead ensures smooth transitions between tasks without feeling bored or overwhelmed. Guideline: Consider making a list of potential activities so you don't waste time deciding later. Step 3: Catch Up On Personal Projects Use this time to work on personal projects such as writing, painting, crafting, etc. These pursuits allow creativity to flow while providing a sense of accomplishment. Step 4: Indulge in Self-Care Routines Treat yourself to spa day at home by taking a warm bath, doing facial treatments, or styling your hair. Pampering oneself boosts confidence and reduces stress levels. Step 5: Get Active Exercise releases endorphins which improve overall wellbeing. Try workout routines available online, dance along to music videos, practice yoga, or go for a run outside if weather permits. Step 6: Explore New Hobbies Try out new things like learning a musical instrument, practicing origami, coding, cooking exotic dishes - anything that piques your curiosity. The internet offers numerous resources for beginners. Step 7: Read Books or Watch Movies Reading expands knowledge and imagination; watching movies provides entertainment. Choose genres based on your preferences. Don't forget to grab snacks! Step 8: Listen To Music Create playlists based on different moods - happy, sad, energetic, relaxed - then listen and sing along. You could even learn lyrics to expand your repertoire. Step 9: Organize & Declutter Organizing spaces brings clarity and peace. Sort through items, discard unnecessary stuff, clean surfaces, and arrange objects systematically. Step 10: Connect With Loved Ones Call friends or relatives, text them, video call, send emails, or write letters. Stay connected despite being physically apart. Step 11: Reflect And Journal Reflect on thoughts, feelings, goals, achievements, challenges faced recently. Writing down experiences helps process emotions better and gain perspective. Step 12: Rest & Relaxation Lastly, remember rest is crucial too. Whether it's taking a power nap, meditating, listening to calming sounds, giving yourself permission to relax increases productivity and happiness. Conclusion: Remember having fun doesn't necessarily mean going out or socializing. It's about finding joy in simple pleasures within our reach. Utilize these steps wisely to transform 'alone time' into 'fun time.' If we stacked these chairs together they wouldn't stand on their own. The process varies slightly depending on which platform you're playing on - PlayStation, Xbox, or PC - but generally involves accessing your friend list within the game's social menu and adjusting your privacy settings accordingly. Here's our comprehensive guide on blocking friends in Need for Speed: **Platform-Specific Instructions:** 1. **PlayStation (PS4 & PS5):** * Launch Need for Speed and sign in to your profile. * Press the Options button on your controller while on the main menu to open the Function Menu. * Navigate to 'Social' > 'Friends'. * Find the friend you want to block and press X to view their profile. * Select 'Block User' at the bottom right corner of their profile page. * Confirm by selecting 'Yes' when prompted. This will remove them from your Friend List and prevent them from sending messages or invites until you unblock them. 2. **Xbox One & Series X|S:** * Start Need for Speed and log into your gamertag. * On the main screen, move over to 'Online', then select 'My Crew'. * Scroll down to find the friend you want to block, highlight their name, and press A. * From the dropdown menu, choose 'Block Player'. * When asked for confirmation, hit 'Yes'. They won't be able to send messages or invite you again unless you decide otherwise. 3. **PC (Origin / Steam):** * Open Need for Speed and ensure that you are logged into your account. * Click on the Social tab located near the top center part of the screen. * Go to the 'Friends' section on the left side panel. * Locate the friend you intend to block, click on their username, and choose 'View Profile'. * At the bottom of their profile, there should be a 'Block User' option; click on this. * To confirm, press 'OK'; they will no longer appear in your friend suggestions nor can they interact with you directly through NFS messaging system. **Key Tips and Guidelines:** - Blocking someone does not affect any existing progress made together like shared crews, multiplayer events completed as teammates, etc., but prevents future interactions. - If you ever change your mind about having blocked someone, all three platforms allow you to unblock users via similar steps outlined above - simply look for the 'Unblock User' option instead. - Be aware that blocking someone may impact your relationship outside of gaming too since many games link real identities with profiles nowadays. Therefore, consider whether such action could potentially lead to unnecessary misunderstandings before proceeding. - Remember that even though you cannot see these individuals anymore, mutual friends might still make it possible for them to join lobbies where you are present. So, if total isolation is what you seek, you might need additional measures beyond just hitting 'Block User'. She says "MEC come over here and get your" what on? Title: How to Extend Rental Time of iTunes Movie Rental Introduction: iTunes movie rentals are an excellent way to watch movies without having to purchase them outright. However, sometimes you might need more time than what is initially provided to finish watching your rental. This tutorial will guide you through extending the rental period of an iTunes movie so that you can have ample time to enjoy your film. Step 1: Open iTunes Application To begin with, launch the iTunes application on either your Mac or PC by clicking its icon from the desktop or searching for it via Spotlight (Mac) or Start Menu (PC). Make sure your device meets the minimum system requirements for using iTunes. Key Tip: Keep your Apple ID credentials handy as they'll be required later in this process. Step 2: Access Your Library Once opened, navigate to the top left corner of the screen where you'll find the 'Library' tab. Clicking on this will give you access to all the content associated with your account including Movies. Step 3: Locate the Rented Movie From the drop-down menu under 'Library', select 'Movies'. Scroll down until you locate the movie you wish to extend the rental period for. If you cannot see the movie, ensure that it has been downloaded onto your device; otherwise, it won't appear in your library. Step 4: Check Current Expiry Date Before proceeding, check when your current rental expires. You can do this by right-clicking (or Ctrl+Click on macOS) on the selected movie and choosing 'Get Info'. A new window should pop up showing details about the movie including its expiration date at the bottom beneath 'Rent Expires'. Step 5: Initiate Extension Process Still within the Get Info section, click on 'OK' to close the window. Now hover over the displayed play button; instead of pressing play, press and hold the Option key (Alt key on Windows). An additional option labelled 'Redeem Code' should now be visible. Click on it. Step 6: Enter New Redeemalbe Code In the redeem code box that appears, enter your newly purchased redemption code. These codes can be bought directly from Apple's website or other authorized retailers. After entering the code, hit 'Redeem'. Step 7: Confirm Successful Extension After successful entry, a confirmation message will display indicating the extension of your rental period. Close the dialog boxes and return to your library where you'll notice the updated expiration date below the extended movie. Guidelines & Key Tips: - Remember, once you start playing a rented movie, you usually have only 48 hours to complete viewing before it automatically disappears from your library. So plan accordingly after extending the rental period. - Only one extension per movie is allowed. Therefore, if you think you may need more than double the original rental duration, consider purchasing the movie instead. - Be aware that prices for extensions vary depending on the movie's price point during regular sale. Higher priced films typically cost more to extend. - It's important to note that some promotional offers or sales might restrict users from being able to extend their rentals. Always read terms and conditions carefully before making any purchases or attempting extensions. And um it scared and turned it back into its normal person so um it was fun for them. Use barriers such as tongs, papers, spoons or gloves to prepare and serve ready-to-eat foods. - Get the food to be fixed from storage, the stove, the cooler or freezer. Take a little food out at a time, and keep the rest hot or cold until you are ready to work with it. Prepare potentially hazardous foods just before you need them. - Don't let the temperature of the food stay in the "Danger Zone." Use a metal stem or digital thermometer to check temperatures while cooking food to make sure that it gets done all the way inside. Different foods have to reach different temperature degrees to be done or safe. All foods need to be cooked for a minimum of 15 seconds at the required temperatures and roasts need to be held at 130 degrees F for 112 minutes before serving. The metal stem thermometer measures the inside, or internal, temperature of the food. A thermometer that works best shows a range of 0 F to 220 F. The only way you can be sure that the food is cooked enough is to use a metal stem thermometer placed in the center of the food, even if you also use a thermostat to control the temperature in the oven. When is the food cooked safe? Here are a few examples of potentially hazardous food and how hot they must be to be safe. They can be hotter, but they must be at least this hot to kill germs: - Poultry and Stuffing: 165 F - Pork: 145 F - Beef, Lamb and Seafood: 145 F - Rare Beef: 130 F - Hamburger (ground beef): 155 F You must place the thermometer in the thickest part of the meat or in the center of the food to get a true reading. (Do not touch a bone with the stem of the thermometer.) - All poultry, all food made from poultry, all stuffed meats, and the stuffing in them must reach 165 F or hotter to destroy Salmonella and other bacteria. - Hamburger (ground beef) must be cooked to 155 F. This includes all kinds of hamburger such as taco meat and meatloaf, as well as hamburger patties. - Pork and all foods made from pork must cook to at least 145 F to prevent trichinosis, a very serious illness. - Fish, seafood's, all foods made with seafood, and all other meats, such as beef and lamb, must be cooked to 145 F or hotter to kill the bacteria that cause foodborne illness. Some people like rare beef, and this is the one meat that can be cooked to only 130 F if it is served right away. No raw meat is really safe to eat. Never cook large roasts, turkeys or stuffed turkeys while they are still frozen. Their big size keeps the insides from cooking to a safe temperature. You must thaw them first so the heat can reach the center of the meat faster. Microwave ovens do not cook evenly; you must stir and turn the food while it cooks to make sure it cooks to the same temperature in every part. Check the food with a metal stem thermometer before you serve it. (Do not keep the thermometer in the food while it is cooking in the microwave oven.) How cold is cool? How hot is warm? Between the time you cook the food and you put away the cooked food in a cooler or freezer, its temperature can fall into the "Danger Zone." You will learn about how to keep cooked foods hot, hot holding, and how to reheat cold food. You will also learn how to get cooked foods cool, and how to keep food cold, cold holding. We begin with cooling hot food the right way. You always take a chance when you have to cool down food. The best way to have safe food is to make it fresh each day, just before you serve it. If you have food that is leftover or made in advance, you must cool it and store it safely. The first rule to remember about cooling: Cool hot food as fast as you can to 41 F or below, past the "Danger Zone." Food that is not cooled fast enough is one of the leading causes of food borne illness. Cooling solid and soft foods Here are the six steps to cool solid and soft foods such as meats, refried beans, rice, potatoes, casseroles, stews, chili and thick soups or chowders: - Wash your hands. - Before you put away any food, you must place it in shallow metal pans, with the food no more than 2 inches deep. - Cut large roasts and turkeys into pieces no larger than 4 inches. - Put all meats and other hot food in the cooler or refrigerator as quickly as you can, right away; do not let the food sit at room temperature for more than 30 minutes. - Do not stack pans; leave space for air to move around them. - Wait until the food has cooled to below 41 F before you cover it. Cooling liquid foods When you cool thin soup, sauces and gravy, you can use shallow 2-inch metal pans, or you can use the ice and water method, called an "ice bath". Remember, you want the food to cool as fast as possible to below 41 F. For shallow pan cooling, quickly put the hot food in metal pans that are wide with low sides; the food must be no more than 2 inches deep. Do not cover the food until it has cooled to 41 F in the refrigerator. It may be hard to carry a shallow pan with thin soup in it. The ice bath method works well for this job. Here are nine steps you take to cool food with an ice bath: - Wash your hands. - Close the drain in a large sink. Place the metal pot or pan of hot food in the sink. - Fill the sink with ice up to the level of food in the pot. - Add cold water to the ice. - Stir the soup or sauce often so that it cools all the way to the center. - Add more ice as the old ice melts. - Check the food temperature with the metal stem thermometer. (Clean he thermometer stem after each use.) - Be sure you have cooled the food from 140 F to 70 F in 2 hours and from 70 F to 41 F within 6 hours. - Put the cooled foods into the refrigerator or freezer. Each refrigeration unit, cold table or cooler must have its own thermometer that gives a true measure of how cold the air is, but you must also check the food with a metal stem thermometer. Air in the cooler must be able to move around the food, so the pans and dishes need to have space between them; do not crowd them. For cold holding, do not let food stand at room temperature because that will allow germs to grow. Store foods in a refrigerator, refrigerated display case, in ice, or other approved method. Always cold hold foods at 41 F or less. Fish, shellfish, poultry, milk and red meat will stay fresh longer if you cold hold them below 41 F. Use the metal stem thermometer to check the food in cold holding, for example, in salad bars, areas where you prepare food, and in coolers. If you use ice to keep the food cold on a salad bar or food display, be sure that the ice comes up to the level of the food that is in the pan or dish. Food must be colder than 41 F when you put it in the ice. Cold hold foods at 41 F or less. There are only three safe ways to thaw foods, and you must plan ahead to allow enough time to do it right: - Thaw food in the refrigerator; it may take a few hours or a few days. This is the best and safest way. Be sure to put meat in a container to catch the meat juices and to keep them from dripping on the food below. - Hold the food under cool, running water, never under warm or hot water. - In a microwave oven; you must then cook it or serve it right away. Never thaw food at room temperature, on a counter or in warm water. These methods let harmful bacteria grow to high numbers (the "Danger Zone").ever thaw food at room temperature, on a counter or in warm water. These methods let harmful bacteria grow to high numbers (the "Danger Zone"). Special rules for cold salads and sandwich spreads You have learned about potentially hazardous food, and how the bacteria grow very easily in them. These foods must not be left at room temperature for even a short time. Foods like potato salad; pasta or macaroni salad egg salad and chicken salad has to be cold enough to keep germs from growing. When you make these foods, start with cold ingredients. - Wash your hands before handling the salad ingredients. - Make cold salads with cold cooked foods such as potatoes, pasta, chicken and eggs; all ingredients should be chilled to 41 F. If you wonder about keeping something cold, keep it cold while you check with a supervisor, the boss or the Health Department. After the food is cooked and ready to serve, keep it warm enough to stop any germs from growing. There is special equipment for this. You must turn on steam tables, soup warmers, and heated surfaces before you need them so that they will be hot enough when you put the cooked food into them. Set the temperature control a little above 140 F, and then check the food with your metal stem thermometer to make sure the food stays at least at 140 F at all times. Stir liquid foods (like soups and gravies) so they don't get cold on top. Covers on the pans will help to keep the heat in and the food warm enough. Do not try to heat cold foods in these warmers. Hot hold food above 140 F. Food that is cooked and then cooled may need to be heated again. When you must reheat food, do it very quickly (within 2 hours) to 165 F. The right way to do this is on the stove burners, or in microwave ovens, convection ovens, or double boilers. Do not use anything that will heat the food slowly, because it takes too long to pass the "Danger Zone." Stir the food to be sure that all parts of it are hot. Then use your metal stem thermometer to check the temperature. Reheat foods to 165 F. What about food left at the table? When a customer leaves food on a plate or at the table, you must throw it away. If you have food like chips, rolls and bread and some of it is left over, you cannot serve it again. Unopened packages of crackers, jelly, candy or sugar may be served again. When the equipment breaks down or power goes off If the electric power goes off, if the water supply is damaged, if there is no hot water, if the sewer or waste system backs up in the drains: - Close the business right away. - Call Public Health at 206-296-4632 for help and advice. If something goes wrong with the stove, the refrigerators, the freezers, the steam tables, salad bar or display coolers, any equipment that keeps the food safe to serve, you must think and act quickly: - Be sure potentially hazardous hot foods stay hot (at least 140 F or more). - Be sure potentially hazardous cold foods stay cold (at least 41 F or colder). If a refrigerator does not work right, the temperature of the food in it can reach the "Danger Zone." Before you move the food to another cooler check its temperature with the metal stem thermometer. If it is still colder than 41 F, move it quickly to a cooler or refrigerator that is OK. If a freezer lets food thaw, check the food temperature with a metal stem thermometer. You can prepare the food, if it is still colder than 41 F. If hot holding equipment like a steam table or soup warmer fails, measure the temperature of the food it was holding. If the food is still hotter than 140 F, you have two choices: - Move the hot food to equipment that is OK and that will keep it hot. - Cool the food quickly using shallow metal pans or an ice bath. You must throw out food that has gotten warmer than 41 F or cooler than 140 F. Do not serve it and do not give it to staff, family or shelters. Call your local Health Department office for help and advice. mhm, and where does a seahorse live? That's probably the only difference recently too. Title: How to Buy Feather Pillows: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Feather pillows are popular choices due to their durability, comfort, and ability to conform to your head and neck, providing excellent support while you sleep. However, buying feather pillows requires careful consideration as they come with pros and cons. This guide will walk you through every essential aspect of purchasing high-quality feather pillows that suit your needs. Step 1: Understand Your Needs Before making any purchase, consider what type of sleeper you are - side, back, or stomach sleeper? Different pillow types cater to different sleeping positions. For instance, if you're a side sleeper, you might need a thicker pillow to maintain proper spinal alignment; whereas, stomach sleepers may prefer thinner ones to avoid straining the neck. Key Tip: Consider allergies! Some people are sensitive to down or have dust mite allergies. If this is the case, opt for hypoallergenic fillings like synthetic down alternatives. Step 2: Choose the Right Fill Power Fill power refers to the amount of space one ounce of down occupies when allowed to reach its maximum loft naturally without external force. Higher fill powers usually mean softer, more luxurious pillows but can be less supportive than lower fill powders. Conversely, lower fill power means denser and firmer pillows which provide better support but could feel too firm for some users. Guideline: As a general rule, choose a higher fill power (500+) for added softness and luxury, especially if you prefer plush surfaces. Lower fill powers (400 or below) offer greater structure and firmness, ideal for those who require strong support. Step 3: Check the Quality of Materials Look at both the outer shell material and inner filling materials. High-quality casing fabrics include cotton, linen, or polyester blends, often treated with stain guards or moisture wicking capabilities. The best fillings should consist of premium goose or duck feathers, ensuring longevity and resilience. Key Tips: * Look for double-stitched seams for enhanced durability. * Ensure there are no quills poking out from the fabric - a sign of poor quality control. Step 4: Decide Between Pre- washed vs Unwashed Unwashed feather pillows tend to fluff up more initially, offering superior cushioning right after unpackaging. On the other hand, pre-washed pillows arrive feeling slightly broken-in, reducing the time needed to 'plump' them up. Pre-washing also removes natural oils present in raw feathers, minimizing potential allergy triggers. Step 5: Compare Prices & Brands Prices vary significantly based on brand reputation, materials used, craftsmanship, and additional features such as adjustable fills. While budget options exist, investing in mid-range or premium brands typically guarantees longer lifespans, improved comfort, and reliable customer service. Key Guideline: Always read reviews before finalizing your decision. They can provide valuable insights into product performance, user satisfaction levels, and common issues. Conclusion: Selecting the perfect feather pillow involves understanding your personal preferences, assessing fill power requirements, verifying material quality, deciding between pre-washed and unwashed versions, and comparing prices across various brands. By following these steps meticulously, you'll end up with a comfortable, durable, and supportive feather pillow tailored specifically to your needs. There are 3 assets for this resource. This is an exciting lesson that teaches nouns and verbs to 2nd grade students in an engaging and fun way! Title: Nora Noun and Victor Verb Grade Level: 2nd Lesson Length: 45 minutes Objective: To teach what nouns and verbs in an exciting and engaging way. Students will be able to demonstrate knowledge of nouns, verbs in present and past tense, and helping verbs. They will also demonstrate how to use them properly to construct a complete sentence. Illinois State Learning Standards: IL.3.A STANDARD: Use correct grammar, spelling, punctuation, capitalization and structure. IL.3.A.1 Construct complete sentences which demonstrate subject/verb agreement; appropriate capitalization and punctuation; correct spelling of appropriate, high-frequency words; and appropriate use of the eight parts of speech. - Two large sheets of white paper, large enough for two human outlines. - Mad Lib - Index cards Anticipatory Set: To open the lesson, I will ask the kids to select nouns and verbs from a list. I will fill this into the Mad Lib story and read it aloud so they can hear the comical story. Sequence of activities: 1) A brief mini-lesson on nouns and verbs will be taught. This mini-lesson will include helping verbs, verbs in the present and past tense, and the verbs be and have. It will also review that a noun is a person, place or thing. 2) The two cut-outs, Victor Verb and Nicky Noun will be introduced. We will begin with Nicky Noun. As the class comes up with ideas of nouns, write them in the appropriate places. For instance, if a child says "nose" write the word nose on the face where the nose would be located. If the class begins to run out of ideas, point at different parts, such as the shoulders, etc. 3) Next, label Victor Verb with verbs. Each verb should be placed in the area where it would be appropriate. For instance, "talk, chew, and sing" would all be put where the mouth is located. 4) While labeling Victor Verb, students will be asked higher-level thinking questions about different types of verbs. For example , if a child says run for the legs, the teacher might ask how could we make this a past tense verb? (expected student response: run) How could I use a helping verb here? (expected student response: was running)* *helping verbs may be difficult for students and require further support.* 5) At this point in the lesson, the teacher should check for understanding using a quick, thumbs-up, thumbs-down method before proceeding to the final activity. If needed, provided a few additional examples of nouns and verbs. 6) To assess student understanding of nouns and verbs, they will be placed in groups of 4-5. Each student will be given a note-card labeled with a helping verb, present tense verb, past tense verb, be or have, or a noun. Each student will be in charge of finding their matching card in the group. For example, the person who has the "ran" card will find the card "past tense verb". 7) After each person in the group has found their part of speech, the group should arrange themselves in a line to form a sentence. They will then say their word and hold up their part of speech in order to read the sentence. Assessment: While the students are matching their word to the parts of speech card, the teacher will be observing the students. The teacher will also use a rubric to assess student understanding of nouns and verbs. The teacher will take brief notes during the group presentation of sentences. Closure: The teacher will bring the students back together and remind them of what we learned today. "Today we learned about nouns and verbs. We learned that a noun is a person, place, or thing. We also learned that a verb is an action word. We talked about verb tenses, (past and present) helping verbs, and the verbs be and have." see attached assessment rubric under"assets" Shall I take the paper off for you or do you want to take it off? w'll what're you doin' with that basket. Classifying an organism is the process of determining its taxonomic rank within the hierarchy of life, which includes domains, kingdoms, phyla, classes, orders, families, genera, and species. This task can be accomplished through observation and analysis of various characteristics such as cell structure, body symmetry, mode of reproduction, and other unique features that define different groups of living things. The following steps will guide you through the process: ### Step 1: Observe General Characteristics Before diving into specific details, start by observing general characteristics like size, shape, color, and habitat of the organism. These observations may provide initial clues about what type of organism you're dealing with. For example, if it has a large shell and lives in water, it might be a mollusk. ### Step 2: Determine Cell Structure One crucial factor in classifying organisms is whether they are unicellular (consisting of one cell) or multicellular (made up of many cells). Unicellular organisms often fall under bacteria, protists, or algae categories. Multicellular ones could belong to any number of animal, plant, or fungi groups. Key Tip: Use a microscope for this step to get a clear view of the organism's cell structure. ### Step 3: Check Body Symmetry The symmetry of an organism can also help narrow down its classification. Animals either have radial symmetry (similar along all axes), bilateral symmetry (mirror image when divided vertically), or asymmetrical bodies. Plants generally do not exhibit these types of symmetries but have their own set of defining structural traits. ### Step 4: Analyze Reproductive System Examine the reproductive system of the organism. Some questions to consider include: Does it reproduce sexually or asexually? If sexually, does it produce eggs or live young? Observing these aspects can further refine your classification efforts. ### Step 5: Identify Distinct Features Look for distinct physical attributes that are characteristic of certain groups. For instance, animals with exoskeletons are likely arthropods; those with spines and backbones could be vertebrates. Similarly, plants with flowers usually belong to the angiosperm group while those without flowers may be gymnosperms or non-vascular plants. Guideline: Be thorough during this stage because sometimes seemingly insignificant features can make a big difference in identification. ### Step 6: Consult Taxonomic Keys Once you've gathered enough information, consult taxonomic keys - tools used by biologists to identify unknown specimens based on sequential yes/no answers regarding observed characters. Many online resources offer interactive digital keys for diverse taxa. ### Step 7: Cross-Reference with Reliable Sources To ensure accuracy, cross-reference your findings with reliable sources such as scientific literature, textbooks, or expert opinions. Remember, self-identification isn't always accurate due to our inherent bias, so seeking external validation is essential. ### Step 8: Review & Revise Your Classification Based on your research, review and revise your preliminary classification. It might take several iterations before arriving at the correct placement. Don't feel discouraged; even professionals frequently revise their classifications as new knowledge emerges. By following these steps meticulously, anyone should be able to classify an organism accurately and confidently. Happy exploring! How 'bout in this other car [toy]. Imagine you're trying to find the average height of all fifth graders in your city. You measure the heights of twenty random fifth graders and get different numbers, so you decide to create a range, or "interval," that you think will likely contain the true average height. This range is called a confidence interval. Now, let's say you built a special machine that measures the heights of many groups of twenty fifth graders over and over again. Each group would give you a slightly different interval because every group has unique individuals with varying heights. Over time, if you collected A LOT of intervals, around 90% of them would actually include the real, actual average height of all fifth graders in the city! That's what the statement means by saying, "the fraction of calculated confidence intervals (which would differ for each sample) that encompass the true population parameter would tend toward 90%." However, here's where it gets tricky - since you have only one set of measurements, you can't know for sure whether YOUR specific interval includes the true average height with 90% certainty. It's part of the process that works well overall when done repeatedly but doesn't apply directly to any single case. So while it's tempting to believe that your particular interval has a 90% chance of containing the true average height, that's not quite accurate based on the definition of confidence intervals. Instead, just remember that your interval is most likely one of those successful 90% that capture the truth when tested multiple times! You really like that ice cream, don't you? Because I can trap them in there. Look at that you lifted the tacos. 'Cause it amazes me that she sits in this one spot and doesn't move around to play with all these toys.
